In [1]:
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
from pathlib import Path
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import plotly.express as px
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA
In [2]:
file_path= Path.cwd()
file_path
Out[2]:
WindowsPath('c:/Users/komet/Data-Vis-CA1/Carlson')

Read CSV Files

In [3]:
file_name="season-1920.csv"
data_19_20 = pd.read_csv(file_path / file_name)
file_name="season-2021.csv"
data_20_21 = pd.read_csv(file_path / file_name)
file_name="season-2122.csv"
data_21_22 = pd.read_csv(file_path / file_name)

Combine the data

In [4]:
# Empty array
tables = []

tables = pd.concat([data_19_20, data_20_21, data_21_22])
tables=pd.DataFrame(tables)
tables

1140 rows × 22 columns

seperate Home teams from Away teams

Get home team data

In [5]:
home = pd.DataFrame({
    "team": tables["HomeTeam"],
    "matches": 1,
    "goals": tables["FTHG"],
    "wins": (tables["FTR"] == "H").astype(int),
    "draws": (tables["FTR"] == "D").astype(int),
    "losses": (tables["FTR"] == "A").astype(int),

    "halftime_goals": tables["HTHG"],
    "halftime_wins": (tables["HTR"] == "H").astype(int),
    "halftime_draws": (tables["HTR"] == "D").astype(int),
    "halftime_losses": (tables["HTR"] == "A").astype(int),

    "shots": tables["HS"],
    "shots_on_target": tables["HST"],
    "fouls_commited": tables["HF"],
    "corners": tables["HC"],
    "yellow_cards": tables["HY"],
    "red_cards": tables["HR"]
})

Get away team data

In [6]:
away = pd.DataFrame({
    "team": tables["AwayTeam"],
    "matches": 1,
    "goals": tables["FTAG"],
    "wins": (tables["FTR"] == "A").astype(int),
    "draws": (tables["FTR"] == "D").astype(int),
    "losses": (tables["FTR"] == "H").astype(int),

    "halftime_goals": tables["HTAG"],
    "halftime_wins": (tables["HTR"] == "A").astype(int),
    "halftime_draws": (tables["HTR"] == "D").astype(int),
    "halftime_losses": (tables["HTR"] == "H").astype(int),

    "shots": tables["AS"],
    "shots_on_target": tables["AST"],
    "fouls_commited": tables["AF"],
    "corners": tables["AC"],
    "yellow_cards": tables["AY"],
    "red_cards": tables["AR"]
})

Concatenate home team and away team data

In [7]:
fixtures_results = pd.concat([home, away])

Save fixtures data as CSV file

In [8]:
fixtures_results.to_csv('fixtures.csv', index=False)

fixtures_results

Sum each team's values

In [10]:
combined_data = fixtures_results.groupby("team").sum().reset_index()

dtype: int64

Get Refree data, using group by

In [13]:
tables['TotalGoals'] = tables['FTHG'] + tables['FTAG']
tables['TotalFouls'] = tables['HF'] + tables['AF']
tables['TotalYellow'] = tables['HY'] + tables['AY']
tables['TotalRed'] = tables['HR'] + tables['AR']

ref_summary = tables.groupby('Referee').agg(
    Matches=('Date', 'count'),
    Goals=('TotalGoals', 'sum'),
    Fouls=('TotalFouls', 'sum'),
    Yellow_Cards=('TotalYellow', 'sum'),
    Red_Cards=('TotalRed', 'sum'),
    Home_Win_Pct=('FTR', lambda x: (x == 'H').mean()),
    Away_Win_Pct=('FTR', lambda x: (x == 'A').mean())
).reset_index()

Display sample data

ref_summary.head()

save referees and teams data as csv files

In [16]:
combined_data.to_csv('teams_summary.csv', index=False)
ref_summary.to_csv('ref_summary.csv', index=False)
In [17]:
numeric_cols = combined_data.select_dtypes(include=[np.number]).columns

Univariate analysis

Check for numerical values distribution for teams

In [18]:
for col in numeric_cols:
    sns.violinplot(y=combined_data[col])
    name= col  + " Analyis through out 3 seasons"
    plt.title(name)
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 8))
    plt.show()

checking the number of matches played by each team

In [21]:
fig = px.histogram(fixtures_results, x='team', color='team',
             title='Matches Played distribution '
             )

# Show the plot
fig.show()

checking the number of matches Refereed by each referee

In [22]:
fig = px.histogram(tables, x='Referee', color='Referee',
             title='Matches Refereed distribution'
             )

# Show the plot
fig.show()

Multivariate and Bivariate analysis

investigating the relationship other values have with goals

In [23]:
numeric_df = combined_data.select_dtypes(include=[np.number])
sns.pairplot(
    numeric_df,
    x_vars=[col for col in numeric_df.columns if col != "goals"],
    y_vars=["goals"],
    kind="reg",
    height=3,
    aspect=1
)

plt.show()
In [24]:
fig = px.box(fixtures_results, x='team', y="goals", color='team',
             title='goals per match vs teams '
             )

# Show the plot
fig.show()

Investigating how goals can influence wins

In [25]:
fig = px.scatter(data_frame=combined_data, x="wins", y="goals",  title="wins vs goals",
            size="losses", color="team", hover_name="team", 
            hover_data=['wins','draws','losses', 'yellow_cards', 'halftime_wins']
)
fig.show()

Investigating how Fouls can influence Cards

In [26]:
fig = px.scatter(data_frame=ref_summary, x="Yellow_Cards", y="Fouls",  title="wins vs goals",
            size="Red_Cards", color="Referee", hover_name="Referee", 
            hover_data=['Matches','Home_Win_Pct','Away_Win_Pct']
)
fig.show()

PCA¶

In [27]:
temp=combined_data
X = temp.drop(columns=["team"])
In [28]:
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)
In [29]:
pca = PCA(n_components=2)   # reduce to 2 components for visualization
principal_components = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)

pca_df = pd.DataFrame(
    principal_components,
    columns=["PC1", "PC2"]
)

pca_df["team"] = temp["team"]
In [30]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 7))

plt.scatter(pca_df["PC1"], pca_df["PC2"])

for i, team in enumerate(pca_df["team"]):
    plt.text(pca_df["PC1"][i], pca_df["PC2"][i], team)

plt.xlabel("Principal Component 1")
plt.ylabel("Principal Component 2")
plt.title("PCA of Team Performance")
plt.show()

PCA Loadings

In [31]:
loadings = pd.DataFrame(
    pca.components_.T,
    columns=["PC1", "PC2"],
    index=X.columns
)

print(loadings)

                      PC1       PC2
matches          0.308187  0.120542
goals            0.285844 -0.230193
wins             0.282286 -0.241738
draws            0.222484  0.265437
losses           0.076910  0.499627
halftime_goals   0.281989 -0.232146
halftime_wins    0.282753 -0.219696
halftime_draws   0.261532  0.202894
halftime_losses  0.101269  0.496675
shots            0.305719 -0.117244
shots_on_target  0.302469 -0.151910
fouls_commited   0.294750  0.175720
corners          0.305006 -0.098075
yellow_cards     0.266504  0.233051
red_cards        0.119212  0.179773
In [32]:
print(pca.explained_variance_ratio_)

[0.66326251 0.21523244]

  • PC1 explains 66.33% of the total variance

  • PC2 explains 21.52% of the total variance

PC1 represents overall team activity and competitive intensity, capturing 66% of variance. PC2 represents performance success, separating winning teams from losing teams. Together, the two components explain nearly 88% of total variation, meaning team performance can largely be summarized along strength and success dimensions

In [33]:
temp_ref=ref_summary
X = temp_ref.drop(columns=["Referee"])
In [34]:
scaler = StandardScaler()
X_scaled = scaler.fit_transform(X)
In [35]:
pca = PCA(n_components=2)   # reduce to 2 components for visualization
principal_components = pca.fit_transform(X_scaled)

pca_df = pd.DataFrame(
    principal_components,
    columns=["PC1", "PC2"]
)

pca_df["Referee"] = temp_ref["Referee"]
In [36]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 7))

plt.scatter(pca_df["PC1"], pca_df["PC2"])

for i, team in enumerate(pca_df["Referee"]):
    plt.text(pca_df["PC1"][i], pca_df["PC2"][i], team)

plt.xlabel("Principal Component 1")
plt.ylabel("Principal Component 2")
plt.title("PCA of Referee strictness")
plt.show()

PCA loadings

In [37]:
loadings = pd.DataFrame(
    pca.components_.T,
    columns=["PC1", "PC2"],
    index=X.columns
)

print(loadings)

                   PC1       PC2
Matches       0.449496  0.019993
Goals         0.446846  0.020834
Fouls         0.449118  0.015468
Yellow_Cards  0.447142  0.019222
Red_Cards     0.428898  0.016893
Home_Win_Pct  0.104699 -0.681995
Away_Win_Pct  0.041525  0.730175
In [38]:
print(pca.explained_variance_ratio_)

[0.69679851 0.17974695]

  • PC1 (69.7%) = Overall officiating intensity / strictness

  • PC2 (18%) = Home-dominant vs Away-dominant match outcomes

  • Together, these two dimensions explain nearly 88% of variation, meaning referee differences are mainly about how active/strict they are and secondarily about home vs away outcome patterns.
